commit_message:
Avoid running SamplingServiceTests if sampling feature flag is missing (#135858)

The SamplingServiceTests fail when running in non-snapshot mode. This is
because we do not do any sampling if the sampling feature flag is not
enabled. This adds an assumption to all of those tests that the feature
flag is enabled. It also fixes a potential NullPointerException that
happens if you attempt to get the sampling service stats with the
sampling feature flag disabled. Closes #135808  Closes #135809 Closes
#135810 Closes #135811

issue_title: Expose all KqlQueryBuilder parameters in ES|QL KQL function
issue_body:
### Context

- Bug [#135772](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/issues/135772) has been resolved, making the KQL function case sensitive by default (consistent with Kibana and documentation).
- Some users may want to perform case-insensitive queries on keyword fields ([see this comment](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/issues/135772#issuecomment-3358042003)).
- The query DSL implementation of KQL supports multiple optional parameters (`case_insensitive`, `default_field`, `time_zone`, and `boost`).
- The ES|QL `kql` function currently relies on the query DSL but these parameters are not exposed through the ES|QL function

### Acceptance Criteria

- Users can specify all KQL query parameters supported by the underlying KqlQueryBuilder in the ES|QL KQL function:
  - `case_insensitive` (boolean): Control case sensitivity for field names and keyword/text terms
  - `default_field` (string): Default field pattern when no field is specified in the query
  - `time_zone` (string): Time zone for interpreting date literals
  - `boost` (float): Query boost for relevance scoring
- If parameters are not specified, the default behavior matches the underlying KqlQueryBuilder defaults
- Documentation clearly explains all available parameters and their behavior
- Parameter validation matches the query DSL implementation

### Proposed Syntax

Allow optional named parameters for the function:

```esql
| WHERE KQL("title: value", {"case_insensitive": true})
| WHERE KQL("timeout", {"default_field": "logs.*", "time_zone": "Europe/Paris"})
| WHERE KQL("error", {"case_insensitive": true, "boost": 2.0})

### Elasticsearch Version

8.19.3

### Installed Plugins

None

### Java Version

_bundled_

### OS Version

ECH Hosted

### Problem Description

A very simple set of ES|QL searches is triggering long running tasks that are not cancellable (even after cancelling, the runtime keeps going up).

### Steps to Reproduce

This does not reproduce consistently. I have no idea why it happens for some searches and not others. It's new in 8.19.3 from 8.15.x though.

### Logs (if relevant)

Here is an example of the ES|QL search task that is "open" for 11 days:

```http
GET /_tasks/9aB-DY70TdWnJja1jyfSYA:2108421440
```

```
{
  "completed": false,
  "task": {
    "node": "9aB-DY70TdWnJja1jyfSYA",
    "id": 2108421440,
    "type": "transport",
    "action": "indices:data/read/esql",
    "description": """FROM my-index-alias
| WHERE type == "my-type" AND cluster_id == "my-cluster-id"
| WHERE timestamp >= TO_DATETIME(1758269383313) AND timestamp < TO_DATETIME(1758270283313)
| STATS max_ts = MAX(timestamp), total_fractions = MAX(total_fractions)
  BY transaction_id, index, node_id
| STATS max_ts = MAX(max_ts), slices_agg = COUNT(*), total_fractions = MAX(total_fractions)
  BY transaction_id, index
| SORT max_ts DESC, transaction_id ASC
| LIMIT 1000
""",
    "start_time_in_millis": 1758270343508,
    "running_time_in_nanos": 1152741808301307,
    "cancellable": true,
    "cancelled": true,
    "headers": {
      "trace.id": "a2d3556e6f18273ef5312371e443066a"
    }
  }
}
```

The alias matches an ILM-based set of time series indexes. In this case, it's two small indexes (around 30 MB each and only around 300K documents total).

As a note about the runtime going up even though it's cancelled:

```
{
  "completed": false,
  "task": {
    "node": "9aB-DY70TdWnJja1jyfSYA",
    "id": 2108421440,
    ...
    "start_time_in_millis": 1758270343508,
    "running_time_in_nanos": 1156539887452789,
    "cancellable": true,
    "cancelled": true,
    "headers": {
      "trace.id": "a2d3556e6f18273ef5312371e443066a"
    }
  }
}
```
commit_message:
Handle index deletion while querying in ES|QL (#137702)

If the index is deleted while querying, ES|QL might try to get the index 
routing from the latest cluster state. This could throw an
index-not-found exception, which is not handled properly, causing the
user to never receive a response, even though execution has completed.
This also results in a dangling task in the task manager.

Relates #126653
Closes #135863

issue_title: Wrong/missing error when supplying username but not password or vice versa for reindex-from-remote
issue_body:
### Elasticsearch Version

Latest

### Installed Plugins

_No response_

### Java Version

_bundled_

### OS Version

N/A

### Problem Description

If you send a reindex-from-remote request and you specify `source.remote.username` but omit the `password`, you get a 500 response with an error message reporting a null pointer exception. This should be a 400 with a description of the user error. (If assertions are enabled, an assertion kills the node instead.)

If you include the `password` but not the `username`, you'll get a 401 with an error message reporting missing credentials (assuming that the remote cluster requires authentication — if it doesn't, it will succeed). This should also ideally be a 400, though this is less important since at least we're getting a 4**.

The cause is that (a) we fail to catch these invalid requests during request validation, and (b) the `Reindexer` code checks for a non-null username to decide whether to trigger the basic auth pathway, which dereferences the password without checking: https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/blob/745c5c39d66c3e1938d8c176d9075245f3467569/modules/reindex/src/main/java/org/elasticsearch/reindex/Reindexer.java#L171-L179

The simple fix is to add the missing request validation. Consideration will need to be given to the backwards compatibility policy. This definitely seems justified for the `username` but no `password` case where we get a 500; more thought may be needed for the vice versa case where we get a 401.

### Steps to Reproduce

To reproduce the 500 response:

1. Start an ES cluster with `reindex.remote.whitelist` set to `localhost:9200` (or the host and port of your chosen target cluster). If running locally, also disable assertions, e.g. with

```
./gradlew run -Dtests.es.reindex.remote.whitelist=localhost:9200 -Dtests.jvm.argline="-da -dsa"
```

2. Send a request with `username` but no `password`, and your chose host and port:

```
POST /_reindex
{
  "source": {
    "remote": {
      "host": "http://localhost:9200",
      "username": "me"
    },
    "index": "my-remote-index"
  },
  "dest": {
    "index": "my-copied-index"
  }
}
```

3. Observe the following response:
```
{
  "error" : {
    "root_cause" : [
      {
       	"type" : "null_pointer_exception",
	"reason" : "Cannot invoke \"org.elasticsearch.common.settings.SecureStr\
ing.toString()\" because the return value of \"org.elasticsearch.index.reindex.\
RemoteInfo.getPassword()\" is null"
      }
    ],
    "type" : "null_pointer_exception",
    "reason" : "Cannot invoke \"org.elasticsearch.common.settings.SecureString.\
toString()\" because the return value of \"org.elasticsearch.index.reindex.Remo\
teInfo.getPassword()\" is null"
  },
  "status" : 500
}
```

If you change the request to include a `password` but omit the `username`, you'll get this instead:

```
{
  "error" : {
    "root_cause" : [
      {
        "type" : "status_exception",
        "reason" : "body={\"error\":{\"root_cause\":[{\"type\":\"security_excep\
tion\",\"reason\":\"missing authentication credentials for REST request [/]\",\\
"header\":{\"WWW-Authenticate\":[\"Basic realm=\\\"security\\\", charset=\\\"UT\
F-8\\\"\",\"ApiKey\"]}}],\"type\":\"security_exception\",\"reason\":\"missing a\
uthentication credentials for REST request [/]\",\"header\":{\"WWW-Authenticate\
\":[\"Basic realm=\\\"security\\\", charset=\\\"UTF-8\\\"\",\"ApiKey\"]}},\"sta\
tus\":401}"
      }
    ],
    "type" : "status_exception",
    "reason" : "body={\"error\":{\"root_cause\":[{\"type\":\"security_exception\
\",\"reason\":\"missing authentication credentials for REST request [/]\",\"hea\
der\":{\"WWW-Authenticate\":[\"Basic realm=\\\"security\\\", charset=\\\"UTF-8\\
\\"\",\"ApiKey\"]}}],\"type\":\"security_exception\",\"reason\":\"missing authe\
ntication credentials for REST request [/]\",\"header\":{\"WWW-Authenticate\":[\
\"Basic realm=\\\"security\\\", charset=\\\"UTF-8\\\"\",\"ApiKey\"]}},\"status\\
":401}",
    "caused_by" : {
      "type" : "response_exception",
      "reason" : "method [GET], host [http://localhost:9200], URI [/], status l\
ine [HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized]\n{\"error\":{\"root_cause\":[{\"type\":\"securi\
ty_exception\",\"reason\":\"missing authentication credentials for REST request\
 [/]\",\"header\":{\"WWW-Authenticate\":[\"Basic realm=\\\"security\\\", charse\
t=\\\"UTF-8\\\"\",\"ApiKey\"]}}],\"type\":\"security_exception\",\"reason\":\"m\
issing authentication credentials for REST request [/]\",\"header\":{\"WWW-Auth\
enticate\":[\"Basic realm=\\\"security\\\", charset=\\\"UTF-8\\\"\",\"ApiKey\"]\
}},\"status\":401}"
    }
  },
  "status" : 401
}
```

commit_message:
Reindex-from-remote: Validate basic auth params (#136501)

This fixes a bug in the reindex API where it did not correctly validate the request parameters for authenticating with a remote source using basic auth. These require both username and password, and providing only one or the other is a user error.

1. Prior to this change, a reindex request which set `source.remote.username` but not `source.remote.password` would result in a response with HTTP status code 500 (Internal Server Error). This will now result in a response with HTTP status code 400 (Bad Request).

2. Prior to this change, a reindex request which set `source.remote.password` but not `source.remote.username` would normally result in a response with HTTP status code 401 (Unuauthorized). (If the remote cluster does not require authentication, or if an API key or some other form of authentication is provided, the request would succeed, with the password silently ignored.) This will now result in a response with HTTP status code 400 (Bad Request).

The new behaviour more correctly indicates to the user that there is an error in their request.

Closes #135925

issue_body:
### Description

Queries like this are folded incorrectly:
```
ROW a = [1, 2] | STATS a + SUM(a) BY a

< Warning: 299 Elasticsearch-9.3.0-a0c76aad5cebedf41e1c1d876a29d75ee8330603 "Line 1:24: java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: single-value function encountered multi-value"
  a + SUM(a)   |       a
---------------+---------------
null           |1
null           |2
```

The `a +` picks up the multivalued `a`, not the result of the grouping. If you run it with a real document you get the (very surprising) output that we expect:
```
curl -s -v -HContent-Type:application/json -uelastic:password -XDELETE localhost:9200/test
curl -s -v -HContent-Type:application/json -uelastic:password -XPOST localhost:9200/test/_doc?refresh -d'{
    "a": [1, 2]
}'
curl -s -v -HContent-Type:application/json -uelastic:password -XPOST localhost:9200/_query?format=txt -d'{
    "query": "FROM test | STATS a + SUM(a) BY a"
}'
  a + SUM(a)   |       a
---------------+---------------
4              |1
5              |2
```

See https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/issues/134792#issuecomment-3361168090 for why we expect this.

commit_message:
Fix lucene compat tests by keeping asserts disabled (#136094)

These asserts have been enabled for the lucene compat because
`oldVersion` is now correctly set after #134584.
Previously, in the Lucene compatibility tests, version was always 
set to `0.0.0` so the asserts were always disabled.

Additionally, re-enable the tests that were muted because of the issue
